data.title: Banner Corporation: The Upside Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
Way back in September 2023, one company that I came out bullish about was Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR). In case you aren't familiar with the enterprise, it operates a string of bank branch offices and loan production offices that are primarily focused on the western coast of the US. It also has some other operations as well, such as those associated with the mortgage industry. At that time, acknowledged that the enterprise had some shortcomings. This included weaknesses from a deposit perspective. By considering the overall growth of the business from a revenue and profit standpoint, I maintained that it was an attractive prospect worthy of a ‘buy’ rating. From that time through today, the company has achieved some improvement on the balance sheet side of things. When it comes to the income statement, the picture has been admittedly mixed.

Despite these troubles, the high asset quality of the business, combined with how attractively priced shares were back then, ultimately resulted in an upside for shareholders. From the time I rated it a ‘buy’ in September 2023 until the time of this writing, shares are up a whopping 50.8%. That's well above the 31.2% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Looking at the picture now, the company is certainly not doing badly. The stock is a little more expensive than I would like it to be. But with the asset quality, where it currently is, I believe that maintaining a very soft ‘buy’ rating is appropriate at this moment.

Checking back in with Banner Corporation

In case you aren't familiar with Banner Corporation, the company operates as a bank holding firm that is incorporated in the state of Washington. As of the end of its 2024 fiscal year, the firm boasted 135 branch offices and 13 loan production offices. These were primarily focused on the West Coast, specifically Washington, Oregon, and California. The company does have a presence in other western states like Idaho and Utah. From its locations, it provides customers with a wide array of commercial banking services and financial products. These include traditional bank offerings such as deposit accounts, mortgages, loans for commercial business and commercial real estate activities, land and land development loans, construction loans, agriculture business loans, and even SBA loans.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, Banner Corporation has had some rocky results in recent years. Back in 2023, for instance, the company had deposits on its books of $13.62 billion. Those ended up falling to $13.03 billion in 2023. But since then, deposits have been on the rise. Last year, they ended at $13.51 billion. And in the most recent quarter of this year, they were $13.59 billion. I wanted to see how much of its deposit base was brokered. Many banks will take on costly brokered deposits in order to grow. This is not necessarily a bad thing, but it does mean that growth is more expensive. Fortunately, in the most recent quarter, only $75.3 million of the deposits on the company's books were brokered. This represents just 0.6% of the total amount.

We should also be paying attention to the company's loan portfolio. Unlike deposits, these have actually been rising pretty consistently. The firm went from $10.01 billion in 2022 to $11.20 billion last year. And in the most recent quarter, they increased further to $11.28 billion. The largest chunk of the company's loan portfolio involved commercial real estate. That accounted for a full 33% of the amount. Whenever I see such a large exposure to commercial real estate, I have to wonder how much of the exposure involves office assets. After all, office vacancy rates have been rising in the US and any sort of debt tied to them should be considered riskier than most other types of commercial real estate. The good news is that, according to management, only 5.4% of its total loan portfolio involves these types of assets.

Outside of commercial real estate, the greatest concentration that the company has is in commercial loans. This would be 21% of the total. This was followed up by construction loans at 15%, one to four family mortgage loans at 14%, multifamily loans at 8%, consumer loans at 6%, and agricultural loans at the remaining 3%.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve some attention also. Securities would be one example here. In recent years, management has been shifting toward a lower concentration of securities on its books. These have dropped from $3.94 billion in 2022 to $3.10 billion in the most recent quarter. Over the same window of time, cash and cash equivalents have actually increased from $243.1 million to $441.9 million. Meanwhile, debt has been on the decline. After peaking at $665.1 million in 2023, it pulled back to $446.7 million in the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year.

When it comes to the income statement, the picture has not been as robust. Despite the growing balance sheet, the company has seen its net interest income contract from $542.8 million in 2022 to $534.1 million in 2024. This is in spite of the fact that the company has seen a small uptick in its net interest margin from 3.68% to 3.75%. During this time, the company saw a reduction in its provision for credit losses as well. This might all seem paradoxical. But it appears that the primary culprit here was the fact that the enterprise saw more of its deposits shift toward interest-bearing accounts and away from non-interest-bearing accounts. Even as interest-bearing accounts saw an increase from an average balance for each year from $7.83 billion to $8.61 billion, non-interest-bearing deposits shrank from $6.43 billion to $4.65 billion. During times when interest rates are rising, depositors will seek out greater yield. And that is precisely what we had here. The good news is that, for the first quarter of 2025, the net interest income that the company generated was $137.9 million. That was up from $132.4 million and was driven at least in part by a jump in net interest margin from 3.74% to 3.92%.

Non-interest income, meanwhile, has started to improve. After dropping from $75.3 million in 2022 to $44.4 million in 2023, it grew to $66.9 million last year. What's more, the $19.1 million reported in the most recent quarter beat out the $11.6 million reported the previous year rather handily. This recent improvement was driven largely by a $4.9 million loss on the sale of securities last year and by a $1.3 million unfavorable swing associated with the net change in valuation of financial instruments that the company has carried at fair value.

Net income has also been a bit problematic. From 2022 to 2024, it declined from $195.4 million to $168.9 million. But recently, it has started to improve. The strong net interest income and non-interest income readings achieved during the first quarter of this year allowed net profits to grow year over year from $37.6 million to $45.1 million. That's not a huge growth, but it's growth nonetheless.

If we use the historical performance that Banner Corporation achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. The price-to-earnings multiple that it has of 12.8 is, admittedly, above the 6 to 10 ranges that I like to see. But in that chart, you can see how the company stacks up against five similar firms. Only one of the five competitors that I compared it to is cheaper than it is on this basis. In the chart below, you can see that the tables turn a bit. This chart shows the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In this case, Banner Corporation is a bit more expensive, with four of the five competitors being cheaper than it is on a price-to-book basis and three of the five being cheaper on a price-to-tangible book basis. Even so, the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples are not terribly high on an absolute basis.

Normally, I would take this opportunity to downgrade a business like this. But the one thing that has me holding on to a soft ‘buy’ rating is the fact that asset quality is appealing. In the first chart below, you can see the firms' return on assets reading 1.15%. While two of the five firms that I compared it to are higher than it is, the total reading of 1.15% is above the 1% minimum threshold that I would like to see. And in the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 10.17% reading that we have here is above four of the five competitors and is above the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I remain optimistic about Banner Corporation. Even though results have been a bit mixed in recent months, the company has high asset quality and shares are not priced at unreasonable levels. It is certainly not an ideal prospect and is not my top pick in this space. But it is attractive enough, in my opinion, to justify a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Diamond Hill International Strategy Q1 2025 Market Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

International equity markets experienced broad-based gains in the first quarter, as the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index rose by 5.23%. Northern Europe, particularly Germany, posted strong returns driven by optimism about a landmark €1 trillion defense and infrastructure spending package. This unprecedented fiscal initiative represents a marked departure from Germany’s traditional fiscal conservatism. Equity markets embraced the prospect of increased spending in the region, though bond markets reacted cautiously, with yields rising amid concerns about expanding deficits. The long-term impacts of this policy shift remain uncertain, particularly given the varying fiscal conditions across Europe, which may limit the extent to which other nations follow Germany’s path.

Chinese equities also delivered a robust return, propelled by advancements in artificial intelligence. The country’s latest AI innovation, DeepSeek, demonstrated the capability to rival US technologies at significantly lower costs, underscoring the intensifying global AI competition. President Xi Jinping also shifted his stance towards the private sector, publicly emphasizing its vital role in China’s economic growth.

Although fiscal stimulus remained modest and domestic consumption continued to lag, strong export performance provided some balance. However, structural challenges in real estate and demographics persist, and whether recent policy measures can sustainably boost consumer spending remains an open question.

Elsewhere in Asia, equity market returns were mixed. Japan ended the quarter flat, while Australia and India registered modest declines. Australian equities’ performance suffered from the country’s exposure to lower commodity prices, while India’s market faced headwinds from slowing consumer and government spending, compounded by a downturn in IT hiring.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio generated positive returns during the quarter, although it trailed the robust return of the benchmark. Sector performance was led by strength in consumer discretionary and information technology, while the financials and materials sectors were notable detractors. Geographically, Japan and Switzerland positively contributed to performance, while holdings in the United Kingdom and Germany weighed on relative returns.

Chinese technology company Alibaba (BABA, OTCPK:BABAF) emerged as a significant contributor, benefiting from solid earnings results and increased market optimism around its AI initiatives.

The stock price responded favorably to management’s expanded capital investment to bolster its AI capabilities. President Xi’s renewed support for the private sector also signaled a potential easing in regulatory scrutiny. With Alibaba’s core operations continuing to generate strong cash flows and attractive valuations, we remain optimistic about its prospects.

Sweden-based Spotify (SPOT), a global leader in audio streaming, also contributed positively following strong fourth-quarter and annual results. The company reported continued user growth, alongside notable margin improvements, which exceeded market expectations. Given the stock’s strong appreciation over the past two years, we are managing the position accordingly.

French aerospace leader Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY) performed strongly, supported by robust demand for aftermarket aerospace products and services. Its position as a leading supplier for narrow-body aircraft engines provides a resilient, recurring revenue stream from maintenance and parts replacement. Recent upticks in aircraft usage and signs of gradual production increases further enhance Safran’s outlook as a durable compounder with sustainable competitive advantages.

Conversely, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM, OTC:TSMWF) detracted from performance despite solid fundamentals.

Geopolitical concerns around Taiwan and broader volatility in the semiconductor cycle negatively influenced investor sentiment. Although near-term pricing pressure and inventory adjustments are standard in the industry, TSMC remains strategically essential to global semiconductor manufacturing, and we continue to view its long-term growth trajectory positively.

Global commodity company Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF)(OTCPK:GLNCY) underperformed despite stable financial results, reflecting investor concerns about commodity price volatility and uncertainty surrounding Chinese industrial demand. Nonetheless, Glencore’s diversified business model, combining its mining activities and commodity trading operations, provides resilience. We maintain our conviction in Glencore’s long-term positioning in copper and coal and management’s demonstrated ability to allocate capital across various market environments.

UniQure (QURE), a Netherlands-based gene therapy company, also detracted due to regulatory uncertainties stemming from recent changes at the US FDA, potentially slowing drug approvals. While these developments weighed on sentiment, we believe UniQure’s robust platform, advanced manufacturing capabilities and promising pipeline offer considerable long-term value not yet reflected in its share price.

Portfolio Activity

New idea generation was robust during the quarter, and we added eight new positions to the portfolio. There was not one common theme with our additions, as each looks to be an attractive long-term opportunity that stands on its own merits. Concurrently, we exited five positions to redeploy capital into higher-conviction opportunities or because their valuations approached our intrinsic value estimates.

We initiated a position in British American Tobacco (BTI)(OTCPK:BTAFF), a global leader in traditional tobacco products. Despite regulatory headwinds, the company’s robust free cash flow, strong dividend profile and undervalued stake in its Indian subsidiary offer an attractive risk-reward.

Similarly, we added Imperial Brands, another leading global tobacco company distinguished by its strong cash flow generation, which has allowed it to return capital to shareholders aggressively. We don’t believe the current stock price reflects the resilience of its cash flows amidst global uncertainty and the growth of its next-generation tobacco products.

We also established a position in Toyo Suisan Kaisha (OTCPK:TSUKY)(OTCPK:TSUKF), the maker of the popular Maruchan brand of affordable,

staple food products such as ramen noodles. Toyo Suisan’s resilient demand profile across economic cycles, healthy balance sheet, improving capital allocation and expansion opportunities in North American markets offer a unique blend of defensive stability and growth potential.

The North West Company (OTCPK:NNWWF) was another notable addition. As a dominant retailer serving remote and underserved communities in Canada, Alaska and the Caribbean, it benefits from geographic insulation, consistent consumer demand and dependable cash flows, which we believe, as long-term investors, are undervalued in the current stock price.

Melrose Industries (OTCPK:MLSPF), a UK-based aerospace and defense company, was purchased due to the cash flow growth potential from its aerospace Risk and Revenue Sharing Partnerships (RRSPs). These partnerships generate cash flow for Melrose over decade-long periods as engines and parts are serviced for maintenance. Cash flow is typically lower in the early years of the partnership but rises in the later years as more maintenance is needed. Additionally, Melrose’s Structures division is undergoing a multi-year margin expansion plan and investment in additive manufacturing (3D printing) technology, which could enhance profitability.

We initiated a position in Big Yellow Group (OTCPK:BYLOF), the UK’s premier self-storage provider. While the business faces temporary occupancy softness, its strong long-term demand dynamics, robust balance sheet and attractive discount to net asset value (NAV) offer a compelling entry point into one of Europe’s highest-quality real estate businesses.

Boardwalk REIT (OTCPK:BOWFF), a Canadian multi-family residential property operator, was also added. Its strategic positioning in regions with robust employment growth and constrained housing supply supports stable occupancy and rent increases. Coupled with conservative financial management and a significant discount to NAV, we believe Boardwalk represents a compelling opportunity.

Lastly, we added Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY)(OTCPK:HTHIF), the Japanese conglomerate, which is executing a multi-year transformation focused on divesting non-core assets and enhancing capital allocation towards higher-margin, growth-oriented businesses in power grids, IT services and consulting. We believe the stock price does not reflect the significant upside potential resulting from these strategic shifts and the growth of its end markets.

On the exit side, we sold Credicorp (BAP), Peru’s largest financial holding company, as the stock reached our estimate of intrinsic value. Julius Baer (OTC:JBPCF), a Switzerland-based global private bank and wealth manager, Ambev (ABEV), the largest brewer in Latin America and GSK (GSK)(OTCPK:GLAXF), a global pharmaceutical company, were all sold to fund higher conviction opportunities elsewhere. Lastly, the Japanese trading house Mitsubishi was sold in favor of other Japanese investments with better long-term business prospects.

Market Outlook

Although international markets started the year strongly, significant uncertainties remain. Growing unpredictability in global trade policy, especially concerning US tariffs and trade relations, poses risks to global supply chains and may pressure companies reliant on international production networks.

Germany’s increased fiscal spending could signal the beginning of a broader capital investment cycle across Europe, but the scale and sustainability of such a shift remain unclear. While markets have initially responded positively to spending announcements, cautious corporate earnings guidance suggests limited near-term visibility and tempered expectations.

We believe capital flows could increasingly shift from US markets towards international equities, driven by compelling valuations and structural improvements. Attractive valuations persist in markets such as Japan, South Korea and many European countries. Additionally, a weakening US dollar, influenced by evolving trade policies and reshoring trends, may further support a shift in performance leadership to international equities.

Our investment strategy remains grounded in disciplined bottom-up analysis. We seek undervalued companies where we identify significant divergence between current stock prices and the business’s intrinsic value over the long term.

data.title: Top Of The Class: Why You Should Consider Pearson For Your Portfolio
data.description:
On March 20th of this year, President Donald Trump signed an executive order to facilitate the transfer of federal authority over education in the United States to the individual states. Trump called on "U.S. Education Secretary Linda McMahon to 'take all necessary steps to facilitate the closure of the Department of Education and return authority over education to the States and local communities.'" Interestingly, "lawmakers passed the 10th Amendment to the Constitution... This is the basis for making education a function of the states." That being said, the federal government has historically played a minor role in education. In fact, the federal government did not issue any major educational policies until the 1960s -- although its role has grown significantly since then. During that decade, several "key acts and policies that have shaped the education system in America" were passed (specifically in 1965), including the Elementary and Secondary Education Act (ESEA) and the Higher Education Act (HEA). ESEA helped give a boost to public education through federal funding, "which benefited schools with low-income students," while HEA primarily focused on higher ("post-secondary") education through financial aid and "other support to help students go to college."

President Trump has stated that the core functions of the Department of Education, which were "establish[ed] under the Carter administration over four decades ago," will be preserved. These core functions, which are "... Pell Grants, Title I funding, and providing funding and resources for students with disabilities," will now be managed by other parts of the government. Trump has cited both the department's bloated budget and "low student test scores as reason[s] to close the department." How exactly will this affect our country's education system and future generations?

In a speech given just before signing the executive order, President Trump succinctly summarized the problem:

After 45 years, the United States spends more money on education by far than any other country, and spends, likewise, by far, more money per pupil than any country... But yet we rank near the bottom of the list in terms of success. That’s where we are — like it or not — and we’ve been there for a long time.”

- Trump signs order closing Education Department to 'maximum extent appropriate by Naaz Modan on March 20, 2025, Higher Ed Dive

This is an understandably frightening and unknown situation for both students and their families; however, private citizens and companies are stepping up to help guide and advise them. I have witnessed this firsthand, as I have been tutoring for over a decade, both privately through my own tutoring business and for a New York-based prep-for-prep non-profit. The technologies have advanced significantly just since I've been tutoring, and I believe there are several innovative companies worth a deeper analysis in the EdTech industry. I follow over half a dozen publicly traded companies in the education space, but several stand out above their competitors. For the purpose of this article, I chose to focus on two: Pearson (NYSE:PSO) and Duolingo (DUOL)... I see it through an old-world vs. new-world lens. While I am also invested in other EdTech companies, I think that these two provide the most compelling contrast.

For some background, Pearson is an established textbook company that has recently expanded into digital learning (through e-textbooks and other online study materials). The move aligns with the company's slogan: "Where learning comes to life: Explore learning for every stage of life with Pearson." The newer Duolingo burst onto the scene in 2012 with its gamified approach (while Pearson's roots trace back to 1836). Although Duolingo started as a language-focused app, in 2023 it expanded to include additional subjects (such as math and music) and has recently also added chess. It is firmly rooted itself into a platform that addresses and strengthens users' cognitive skills, making it attractive to a very broad audience.

Since its development (and beta launch) in 2011, Duolingo has amassed more than 500M learners (100M MAUs) and is the most downloaded educational app on the App Store. Duolingo also reached 10M paying subscribers in the most recent quarter (Q1 2025) while growing revenues 38% from the previous year. For comparison, after launching in July 2021 as a replacement for the Pearson eText app, Pearson+ boasted more than 4.8M learners and 600,000 paying subscribers within a year of launching (according to its 2022 annual report). While Pearson has not released any recent figures, the idea is very appealing: "eTextbooks, study videos, and more -- all in one place."

I believe that now is an opportune time to invest in EdTech companies, as the utilization of their products and services will only continue to increase due to the current education crisis in our country. This crisis includes both the high cost and erratic quality of education, leading to lower reading and writing proficiency and questions regarding its value (especially surrounding higher education).

For those currently using these apps, Duolingo consistently ranks near the top of online rankings (examples I, II, III). Pearson, however, has a different business model, as professors typically assign their textbooks and study packs to a captive audience of students from middle school to college and grad school. Thus, its apps have limited reviews and downloads, but most are rated 4 stars or higher (including Pearson+, Revel by Pearson, Pearson Practice English, and Reader+). It is important to note, however, that several of their apps have also received terrible reviews (Pearson English Portal App, Pearson English Connect), although these are largely based on just a few reviews (fewer than 10 each).

Below is a comparison of the two companies over the past twelve months (as of EOD Wednesday, June 4th):

Company Duolingo Pearson
Revenue (ttm) $811.2M $3.55B
P/S (ttm) 29.99 2.13
Net Income (ttm) $96.75M $434M
EPS (ttm) 2.08 .85
P/E (ttm) 252.89 17.38
P/B 26.11 1.80
Dividend (fwd) N/A 2.05%
Market Cap $23.33B $9.68B
Paid Users 10M (Q1 2025) 4.8M (2022)

While not as exciting as Duolingo and its AI capabilities, Pearson boasts a solid core business and a much better valuation. Currently, Duolingo trades at nearly fifteen times the Price/Sales ratio of Pearson... and Pearson is even more of a value stock when looking at its balance sheet. It is essential to note, however, that Pearson is headquartered in London, so there is always a currency exchange risk (or, potentially, a benefit) that may affect the bottom line.

Pearson finished last year (Q4 2024) with £4.05B in shareholder equity, up slightly (1.83%) from the previous year (Q4 2023). With that said, they also saw large increases in income tax assets (£103M from £15M a year ago) and cash equivalents, excluding overdrafts (£543M from £312M). So while Pearson experienced limited growth in shareholder equity over the past twelve months, the composition of the company's assets has improved, all while returning capital to shareholders. On February 27th of this year, Pearson's board "approved a £350m share buyback program to return capital to shareholders. The program will commence as soon as is practicable." This is in addition to Pearson's dividend, which increased 6% this year to 24.0p per its London Stock Exchange-based share (current yield of 2.21%) -- slightly greater than NYSE-listed PSO's yield of 2.05% ($.30). Despite returning significant capital to shareholders, Pearson's full year 2024 revenue actually decreased 3% to £3,552M. This begs the question: Are there enough positive catalysts to warrant an investment in its stock?

Recent Updates and Forecasts

Preceding its recent first quarter (2025) earnings, Pearson released its Q1 2025 Aide Memoire on March 31st, which brought "... together certain previous public communications made by Pearson as extracted from their original source, which readers may find helpful to consider ahead of Pearson’s Q1 2025 Trading Update on 2nd May 2025." Although no new information was provided, Pearson compiled various forecasts and estimates it had previously made into a single document. It is certainly worth a read -- here are some key takeaways, in my opinion:

- "2025 consensus on the Pearson website, dated 27th January 2025; group underlying sales growth 4.4%. (source: Full Year 2024 Results, RNS 28 February 2025)"

- "2025 consensus on the Pearson website, dated 27th January 2025; group adjusted operating profit of £656m at £:$1.23. (source: Full Year 2024 Results, RNS 28 February 2025)"

- "During the results presentation, the CFO said, 'Turning to 2025. We expect each business unit to grow.' (source: Full Year 2024 Results Transcript, 28 February 2025)"

- "In terms of phasing, as we have seen in other years, growth will generally be weighted to H2, given these known business unit dynamics. So for the Group overall, Q1, which is our smallest quarter, will be muted. H1 will see low single-digit growth, with stronger growth in H2. (source: Full Year 2024 Results Transcript, 28 February 2025)"

- "During the results presentation the CFO said 'You can tell from guidance for 2025, we are going to see margin improvement in 2025.' Group adjusted operating margin improvement will be driven by 'operating leverage on sales growth and then cost efficiencies…offset by investment.' (source: Full Year 2024 Results Transcript, 28 February 2025)"

- "Beyond 2025, Pearson is positioned to deliver a mid-single digit underlying sales growth CAGR, sustained margin improvement that will equate to an average increase of 40 basis points per annum and strong free cash conversion, in the region of 90% to 100%, on average, across the period. (source: Full Year 2024 Results RNS, 28 February 2025)"

- "2025 will be a transitionary year for our K-12 channel as we ramp up our direct sales team selling our proprietary AP®, Dual Enrollment, and CTE materials into US states and school districts. (source: Full Year 2024 Results RNS, 28 February 2025)"

Q1 2025 Results

Approximately one month after compiling their previously stated predictions, Pearson released their first-quarter earnings on May 2nd, in which they declared that: "Pearson is on track to deliver 2025 guidance with expected Q1 result and momentum building for the second half." The company returned to positive sales growth (1%) in the quarter, as it made "[g]ood progress against [its] 2025 strategic priorities." Both the return to growth and business acceleration into H2 were part of the objectives in its Q1 2025 Trading Update. Pearson also made great progress towards other strategic priorities in Q1 as well, including:

- "Expanding professional learning capabilities with [its] new Pearson Skilling Suite"

- "Continuing to lead on the application of innovative technologies, with the launch of an AI-powered Smart Lesson Generator"

- "New contract wins in Enterprise Learning & Skills; including with UK Ministry of Defence"

- "Launch of redefined Pearson brand to embrace the future of learning"

Pearson is firing on all cylinders. Despite its Q1 EPS of $.454 missing estimates, its revenue of $2.28B strongly outperformed expectations. Q1 is coming off the back of an impressive year when Pearson delivered $.81 annual EPS in 2024 (up 23.78% year/year). According to Financial Modeling Prep: "Despite a slight miss in EPS [in Q1], Pearson's strategic initiatives and £350 million share buyback program signal confidence in future growth." Where will PSO go from here?

I believe that Pearson will prioritize both its Enterprise Learning & Skills and Higher Education segments moving forward, though there is an exorbitant amount of potential throughout the company.

Looking at Q1 segment revenue growth, the Higher Education segment continues to stand out as it "led growth with a 6% increase, driven by AI-powered study tools and digital subscriptions":

Pearson is keeping up with the digital transformation in education, offering new platforms and technologies to students. I believe that there is a lot of upside for Pearson, especially as management "reiterat[ed] medium-term guidance for mid-single digit underlying sales growth CAGR and sustained margin improvement that will equate to an average increase of 40 basis points per annum." It will not be an overnight success, but Pearson is already well on its way.

Pearson reported annual revenues of £3552 for FY2024 (excluding its OPM and Strategic Review businesses). Pearson sold its OPM business last year to Private Equity firm Regent LP for a 27.5% cut of profits (for six years) and 27.5% of a potential future sale. The impetus for the sale was Arizona State University ceasing its partnership in June 2023. ASU "represented a third of Pearson's OPM business, [and] triggered a strategic review..." Pearson's OPM business "is credited with helping Arizona State University grow ASU Online into a major force in online education..." In fact, it is one of the 'big five' OPM providers, and "Pearson's sale 'will have an impact on the market...'" Edtech analyst Phil Hill noted that "'In short, this is somewhat of a distressed sale. Get this mess off our hands, you make the big cuts to get it profitable, and we’ll make money only if you can turn it around...'” Hill continued to claim at the time that "Pearson’s business looks to be pivoting into professional education and away from the Higher Education market..." While this move makes sense, Pearson's Q1 2025 segment revenue (above) shows that Higher Education grew revenues by 6% in the quarter, more than any other segment.

Pearson has significant potential for expansion in both its P/E and P/S ratios, while it is simultaneously expected to see its results improve. I believe that all of this makes it an extremely compelling investment.

data.title: Sprout Social: Good Performance, But Profit-Taking Is Appropriate (Downgrade)
data.description:
As stock markets recover to post-YTD gains, I continue to caution investors against being overly optimistic, as valuation multiples are still quite stretched against the possibility of a recession. Emphasizing "growth at a reasonable price" plays has been the cornerstone of my recent investing, and Sprout Social (NASDAQ:SPT) is a great play that checks off a lot of the right boxes.

The software company, which helps companies manage their social media presence, has been a major decliner this year, with shares down ~25% year to date. The stock didn't get much love in 2023 either. Needless to say, the company's social media marketing orientation in a year where many companies are cutting down on growth and marketing-related headcount gives it some risk exposure, but a lot of this risk is already priced into its bargain-basement stock.

I was bearish on Sprout Social for much of 2023 when it was tumbling, but I recently upgraded the stock to a buy rating in April when the stock was at ~$20, before the company's Q1 earnings print. Since then, Sprout Social has achieved solid growth and a surprising boost to full-year guidance, suggesting that the fear surrounding this stock is overstated. But at the same time, the speed of Sprout Social's recent rally from YTD lows has made the stock's valuation a bit less appealing. I'm downgrading Sprout Social back to a neutral position—while I still think this is a stock that can generate meaningful long-term gains, it's no longer a quick-flip play for investors who are looking for near-term gains.

It's important to recognize that the industry that Sprout Social plays in is undergoing major disruption. Companies are responding to weaker consumer spending by pulling back on sales and marketing spend. At the same time, companies are evaluating how they can leverage AI to take headcount away, and the types of social media analytics roles that flowered during the pandemic may be on the chopping block.

Even though Sprout Social slightly boosted its full-year outlook, its management issued cautionary notes on the state of the demand environment, which it does not expect to recover this year. Per CFO Joe Del Preto's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Based on the latest data we gathered during Q1, which reflects their current macro environment, expect us to continue to take a measured view of how we look at the business this fiscal year. As Ryan mentioned earlier, we do not expect the demand environment to improve in fiscal year 2025 from what we experienced in fiscal year 2024. We are closely monitoring this dynamic spending environment impacted by tariffs and federal."

To me, the quick translation is that we should expect to see low double-digit growth through 2025, and Sprout Social may never recover to 20%+ growth again.

To me, Sprout Social's valuation is a mixed bag as well. At current share prices near $22 (which have rallied ~20% from lows just below $19), Sprout Social trades at a market cap of $1.28 billion. After netting off the $101.9 million of cash against $20 million of debt on Sprout Social's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.19 billion.

For FY25, Sprout Social has made the slightest of guidance updates, boosting full-year revenue to $448.9-$453.9 million (11% y/y growth), less than $1 million higher on either end versus a prior view of $448.1-$453.1 million (10-11% y/y) in the prior outlook. It's also expecting $40.7-$45.7 million in pro forma operating income, representing a 9.6% midpoint margin that's ~3 points richer than FY24.

If we also assume Sprout Social's FCF margin (7% in FY24) jumps 3 points y/y (in line with pro forma operating margins), FCF at a 10% margin would indicate $45.1 million of FCF (+52% y/y). This puts Sprout Social's valuation multiples at:

  • 2.6x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 26x EV/FY25 FCF

Yes, Sprout Social appears cheap on a revenue basis: but we also have to keep in mind the fact that Sprout Social's growth looks tipped to decelerate to the low double digits and potentially single digits by next year, given management's cautious demand commentary. As such, the stock needs to start standing on its earnings and FCF multiples, which still look rich.

All in all, I recommend taking advantage of the recent rebound rally to lock in gains on at least part of your position. In my view, we're still due for some stock market volatility throughout this year that will open up a cheaper entry point for Sprout Social down the line.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Sprout Social's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprout Social's revenue grew 13% y/y to $109.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $107.6 million (+11% y/y) by a two-point margin; however, growth did decelerate ~140bps versus 14% y/y growth in Q4, which in turn had decelerated from 20% growth in Q3.

Here's the good news: Sprout Social continues to make good on its promise to migrate to upmarket clients. The company's count of customers that generate over $50,000 in ARR hit 1,766, up 23% y/y. We continue to see evidence of the company's ACV (average contract values) marching upward, hitting $14,961 and rising 16% y/y.

Another positive indicator: Sprout Social's current remaining performance obligations (cRPO), essentially backlog that will convert into revenue within the current 12-month timeframe, grew 21% y/y to $255.8 million. With RPO growth nearly double that of revenue growth, this may be a helpful signal that the company's pace of revenue deceleration may have found a near-term floor.

That being said, we still need to acknowledge the fact that Sprout Social is facing a harsh demand environment, which the company's own management has acknowledged. We also need to understand that Sprout Social prices its products on a per-seat basis, as shown in the price tiers below:

We've seen a constant flurry of companies large and small executing layoffs. Even companies that are succeeding in the AI transition, such as Microsoft (MSFT), announced a layoff impacting 3% of its overall headcount.

Efficiency is the major trend of the day. The types of roles that Sprout Social products support — social media marketing managers — are roles that flourished, and in many cases were created, during the pandemic boom of online advertising and e-commerce. Sprout Social may be seeing a tough demand environment now as many of these roles are on the chopping block or slated to be automated by AI: which will be a direct hit to the company's seat-based subscription business model.

For the time being, the company continues to see respectable margin expansion. Pro forma operating margins rose 530bps y/y to 11.5%:

And yet, we note that Sprout Social scores poorly on a "Rule of 40" basis, with its revenue growth plus its operating margin stacking up to only 24. In other words, while it's true that Sprout Social is cheap from a revenue multiple basis, it's still unable to justify its valuation from an earnings basis, and its poor growth/profitability balance underscores a chunk of that discount.

Key takeaways

I've enjoyed quick, albeit modest gains on my Sprout Social trade, and I'm more than happy to take a chunk of my gains off the table while I wait for shares to consolidate lower. In the long term, I think uncertainty still clouds Sprout Social's future as companies rein in their growth spending and cut headcount. For now, move to the sidelines.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Leverage Overshadows Permian Growth
data.description:
Oil is a notoriously volatile commodity that has not really appreciated in value over the past few decades, when adjusted for inflation. Physical oil has been a lousy long-term investment.

However, shale oil acreage is a completely different matter. As oil recovery rates continue to improve due to technological advances and infrastructure development, the value of the Permian basin continues to increase.

Occidental (NYSE:OXY) is one of the largest leaseholders in the Permian and is best positioned to benefit from this long-term value growth, which likely explains the interest from Warren Buffett.

So far this year, however, OXY stock is underperforming other U.S. oil producers, and we believe their leverage is to blame.

We have previously covered Occidental with a buy opinion in January, but unfortunately, our expectation of tighter sanctions on Iran and Russia did not materialize, and the stock is down 20% since the article was published.

The Permian reserves are growing, despite production

The crown jewel of Occidental is its vast acreage in the prolific Delaware basin of Permian. This deep and multilayered basin presents a lot of technical challenges, however, OXY has been gradually unlocking ever more challenging reserves and increasing production.

Occidental has been adding proved reserves and replacing the barrels that have already been produced. Over the years, the proved reserve base in the Permian did not deplete at all, - it has actually grown.

Despite producing nearly 200 MMbbl of oil per annum on average in the U.S., Occidental has managed to increase its American proved reserve base from 1,466 MMbbl at the end of FY2021 to 1,832 MMbbl at the end of FY2024. Excluding purchases and sales of reserves and the acquisition of Crown Rock, OXY increased its proved reserve base by 133 MMbbl over the past 3 years.
